title: Erectile Dysfunction
oneLiner: Persistent inability to achieve/maintain an erection sufficient for satisfactory sexual performance; common, often multifactorial (vascular, endocrine, psychogenic, drug-induced), and a marker for cardiovascular risk.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cauda equina syndrome** – acute bilateral leg weakness, saddle anaesthesia, and urinary retention. *Findings: perianal pinprick loss, reduced anal tone, post-void residual >200mL.*
- **Pituitary apoplexy** – acute severe headache, visual field defects (bitemporal hemianopia), hypotension, and rapid onset ED. *Finding: diplopia or sudden vision loss + headache.*
- **Spinal cord compression** – acute back pain, leg weakness, sensory level. *Finding: hyperreflexia, Babinski sign, loss of sensation below dermatomal level.*
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Trouble getting or keeping an erection (≥3 months)
  - Reduced rigidity, early detumescence
  - Associated loss of libido, fatigue (if hypogonadism)
  - Premature ejaculation or delayed ejaculation may coexist
- **Key exam findings:**
  - General: BMI, blood pressure (lying & standing), peripheral pulses, abdominal aortic pulsation (aneurysm)
  - Genitourinary: penile plaques (Peyronie’s), testicular atrophy, prostate size/consistency
  - Neurological: perianal sensation, anal tone, lower limb reflexes (if spinal symptoms)
  - Signs of underlying disease: gynaecomastia, loss of body hair (hypogonadism), carotid bruits, diabetic foot
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Psychogenic ED** – younger onset, situational (e.g., with partner but not with masturbation), morning erections intact.
- **Vasculogenic ED** – older, gradual onset, known CVD risk factors (HTN, DM, smoking), absent morning erections.
- **Endocrine ED** – hypogonadism: loss of libido, fatigue, obesity, gynaecomastia; hyperprolactinaemia: galactorrhoea, visual disturbance.
- **Neurogenic ED** – history of spinal injury, pelvic surgery, Parkinson’s, stroke; may have sensory or motor deficits.
- **Drug-induced ED** – beta-blockers, thiazides, SSRIs, spironolactone, recreational drugs (alcohol, cannabis, cocaine).
- **Peyronie’s disease** – palpable penile plaque, curvature during erection, pain with erections.
- **Splitter:** Presence of morning/a.m. erections strongly suggests psychogenic vs organic cause.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Urine dip (glucose, protein), blood pressure (including orthostatic), BMI, penile plaque palpation.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipid profile
  - Testosterone (total + SHBG + calculated free) – morning sample (before 10am)
  - Prolactin (if low libido, galactorrhoea, or hypogonadism)
  - TSH (if other endocrine signs)
- **Imaging:** Not routine. Consider:
  - Penile Doppler ultrasound (if suspected vascular cause, especially young men with low-flow priapism risk)
  - MRI pituitary (if prolactin elevated or symptomatic hyperprolactinaemia/hypogonadism)
  - Spinal MRI (if neurological signs present)
- **Special tests:**
  - Intracavernosal injection test (ICI) + Doppler – assesses vascular integrity; rarely first-line
  - Nocturnal penile tumescence testing – distinguishes psychogenic from organic (if available)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - IIEF-5 (International Index of Erectile Function - 5 items) – score ≤21 suggests ED; lower scores = more severe
  - NICE guidance: use a validated questionnaire (e.g., SHIM) to assess severity and guide management
- **Never-miss test:** **Morning total testosterone** – hypogonadism is treatable and sometimes missed; also check **HbA1c** – undiagnosed diabetes is a common underlying cause.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** No acute treatment needed for ED itself. Address acute underlying issues if present (e.g., cauda equina, pituitary apoplexy). If priapism (sustained erection >4 hours) – this is a urological emergency, treat with aspiration/decompression.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** If admitted for another reason (e.g., stroke, MI, or post-surgery), ensure all medications are reviewed for ED contribution. Start reversible cause management (stop culprit drugs, optimise glucose/lipids, treat hypogonadism). Offer sexual counselling if psychogenic.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifestyle: weight loss, exercise, stop smoking, reduce alcohol
  - Treat underlying conditions: HTN, DM, hyperlipidaemia, hypogonadism
  - Medications:
    - First-line: oral PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil) – “on demand” or daily (tadalafil 5mg)
    - Second-line: intracavernosal alprostadil, vacuum device
    - Third-line: penile prosthesis (urology referral)
  - Psychosexual counselling if indicated
  - If low testosterone + no contraindication (e.g., prostate cancer), consider testosterone replacement therapy (shared care with endocrinology/urology)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** N/A – ED rarely requires inpatient admit; if admitted for other cause, follow standard ward protocol.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Sildenafil** 50mg orally 1 hour before sex; max 100mg once daily. [Check local policy] for dose adjustment with renal/hepatic impairment.
  - **Tadalafil** 10mg orally at least 30 min before sex; max 20mg once daily; or 5mg once daily (for regular sexual activity).
  - **Vardenafil** 10mg orally 25-60 min before sex; max 20mg once daily.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Alprostadil** intracavernosal injection: start 5-10 micrograms, titrate; also intraurethral pellet (125-1000 micrograms).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - PDE5 inhibitors: contraindicated with nitrates (any form) – risk of severe hypotension.
  - Caution with alpha-blockers (especially for BPH) – monitor BP.
  - Severe hepatic/renal impairment: reduce dose or avoid.
  - Testosterone: contraindicated in suspected/known prostate cancer, breast cancer, severe sleep apnoea, polycythaemia.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Sildenafil: severe renal impairment (CrCl <30) – avoid or reduce to 25mg; severe hepatic – avoid.
  - Tadalafil: moderate-severe renal impairment – max 5mg as needed; severe hepatic – avoid.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **PDE5 inhibitor + nitrate** – can cause life-threatening hypotension. Always check nitrate use (in any form: oral, spray, transdermal) before prescribing.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Priapism (with alprostadil injection or high-dose PDE5 inhibitor) – treat as urological emergency
  - Hypotension (nitrate interaction, or overdose)
- **Chronic:**
  - Worsening of underlying cardiovascular risk if left untreated (ED is a sentinel for CVD)
  - Psychological distress, relationship breakdown, depression
sections.Prognosis: - ED itself is not life-threatening; it is a marker for silent ischaemic heart disease and increases all-cause mortality. With treatment (lifestyle + pharmacotherapy), most men achieve adequate erections. Underlying cause (e.g. diabetes) may progress despite treatment.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Ask about morning erections** – if present, psychogenic cause likely; avoid invasive tests.
- **Check testosterone before starting PDE5 inhibitor** – hypogonadism requires replacement, not just PDE5i.
- **Refer all men with sudden onset ED and no clear cause for cardiovascular assessment** – ED may precede MI by 2-3 years.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not prescribe PDE5 inhibitors without checking nitrates** – can cause fatal hypotension.
- **Do not assume all ED is psychogenic** – missing organic causes (diabetes, hypogonadism, vascular disease) delays treatment and increases risk of CVD.
- **Do not start testosterone without ruling out prostate cancer** – PSA, DRE, and risk factors.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** severe headache, visual changes, priapism (erection lasting >4 hours – go to ED), sudden chest pain.
- **Follow-up:** GP in 2-4 weeks to review lifestyle changes and first attempt with medication. Urology/endocrine if no response after 3-4 months of treatment, or if hypogonadism identified.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior:** if suspected cauda equina or spinal cord compression (urgent MRI), pituitary apoplexy (urgent neurosurgery/endocrinology), or priapism (urology on-call).
- **Call ICU:** if PDE5i + nitrate-induced hypotension refractory to fluids; or if pituitary crisis with hypotension, electrolyte disturbance.
- **NEWS2:** use standard track and trigger. ED itself does not cause abnormal vitals; escalate based on underlying condition.

rawMarkdown:
## Erectile Dysfunction
**One-liner:** Persistent inability to achieve/maintain an erection sufficient for satisfactory sexual performance; common, often multifactorial (vascular, endocrine, psychogenic, drug-induced), and a marker for cardiovascular risk.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cauda equina syndrome** – acute bilateral leg weakness, saddle anaesthesia, and urinary retention. *Findings: perianal pinprick loss, reduced anal tone, post-void residual >200mL.*
- **Pituitary apoplexy** – acute severe headache, visual field defects (bitemporal hemianopia), hypotension, and rapid onset ED. *Finding: diplopia or sudden vision loss + headache.*
- **Spinal cord compression** – acute back pain, leg weakness, sensory level. *Finding: hyperreflexia, Babinski sign, loss of sensation below dermatomal level.*

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Trouble getting or keeping an erection (≥3 months)
  - Reduced rigidity, early detumescence
  - Associated loss of libido, fatigue (if hypogonadism)
  - Premature ejaculation or delayed ejaculation may coexist
- **Key exam findings:**
  - General: BMI, blood pressure (lying & standing), peripheral pulses, abdominal aortic pulsation (aneurysm)
  - Genitourinary: penile plaques (Peyronie’s), testicular atrophy, prostate size/consistency
  - Neurological: perianal sensation, anal tone, lower limb reflexes (if spinal symptoms)
  - Signs of underlying disease: gynaecomastia, loss of body hair (hypogonadism), carotid bruits, diabetic foot

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Psychogenic ED** – younger onset, situational (e.g., with partner but not with masturbation), morning erections intact.
- **Vasculogenic ED** – older, gradual onset, known CVD risk factors (HTN, DM, smoking), absent morning erections.
- **Endocrine ED** – hypogonadism: loss of libido, fatigue, obesity, gynaecomastia; hyperprolactinaemia: galactorrhoea, visual disturbance.
- **Neurogenic ED** – history of spinal injury, pelvic surgery, Parkinson’s, stroke; may have sensory or motor deficits.
- **Drug-induced ED** – beta-blockers, thiazides, SSRIs, spironolactone, recreational drugs (alcohol, cannabis, cocaine).
- **Peyronie’s disease** – palpable penile plaque, curvature during erection, pain with erections.
- **Splitter:** Presence of morning/a.m. erections strongly suggests psychogenic vs organic cause.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Urine dip (glucose, protein), blood pressure (including orthostatic), BMI, penile plaque palpation.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipid profile
  - Testosterone (total + SHBG + calculated free) – morning sample (before 10am)
  - Prolactin (if low libido, galactorrhoea, or hypogonadism)
  - TSH (if other endocrine signs)
- **Imaging:** Not routine. Consider:
  - Penile Doppler ultrasound (if suspected vascular cause, especially young men with low-flow priapism risk)
  - MRI pituitary (if prolactin elevated or symptomatic hyperprolactinaemia/hypogonadism)
  - Spinal MRI (if neurological signs present)
- **Special tests:**
  - Intracavernosal injection test (ICI) + Doppler – assesses vascular integrity; rarely first-line
  - Nocturnal penile tumescence testing – distinguishes psychogenic from organic (if available)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - IIEF-5 (International Index of Erectile Function - 5 items) – score ≤21 suggests ED; lower scores = more severe
  - NICE guidance: use a validated questionnaire (e.g., SHIM) to assess severity and guide management
- **Never-miss test:** **Morning total testosterone** – hypogonadism is treatable and sometimes missed; also check **HbA1c** – undiagnosed diabetes is a common underlying cause.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):** No acute treatment needed for ED itself. Address acute underlying issues if present (e.g., cauda equina, pituitary apoplexy). If priapism (sustained erection >4 hours) – this is a urological emergency, treat with aspiration/decompression.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):** If admitted for another reason (e.g., stroke, MI, or post-surgery), ensure all medications are reviewed for ED contribution. Start reversible cause management (stop culprit drugs, optimise glucose/lipids, treat hypogonadism). Offer sexual counselling if psychogenic.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifestyle: weight loss, exercise, stop smoking, reduce alcohol
  - Treat underlying conditions: HTN, DM, hyperlipidaemia, hypogonadism
  - Medications:
    - First-line: oral PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil) – “on demand” or daily (tadalafil 5mg)
    - Second-line: intracavernosal alprostadil, vacuum device
    - Third-line: penile prosthesis (urology referral)
  - Psychosexual counselling if indicated
  - If low testosterone + no contraindication (e.g., prostate cancer), consider testosterone replacement therapy (shared care with endocrinology/urology)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** N/A – ED rarely requires inpatient admit; if admitted for other cause, follow standard ward protocol.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Sildenafil** 50mg orally 1 hour before sex; max 100mg once daily. [Check local policy] for dose adjustment with renal/hepatic impairment.
  - **Tadalafil** 10mg orally at least 30 min before sex; max 20mg once daily; or 5mg once daily (for regular sexual activity).
  - **Vardenafil** 10mg orally 25-60 min before sex; max 20mg once daily.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Alprostadil** intracavernosal injection: start 5-10 micrograms, titrate; also intraurethral pellet (125-1000 micrograms).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - PDE5 inhibitors: contraindicated with nitrates (any form) – risk of severe hypotension.
  - Caution with alpha-blockers (especially for BPH) – monitor BP.
  - Severe hepatic/renal impairment: reduce dose or avoid.
  - Testosterone: contraindicated in suspected/known prostate cancer, breast cancer, severe sleep apnoea, polycythaemia.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Sildenafil: severe renal impairment (CrCl <30) – avoid or reduce to 25mg; severe hepatic – avoid.
  - Tadalafil: moderate-severe renal impairment – max 5mg as needed; severe hepatic – avoid.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **PDE5 inhibitor + nitrate** – can cause life-threatening hypotension. Always check nitrate use (in any form: oral, spray, transdermal) before prescribing.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Priapism (with alprostadil injection or high-dose PDE5 inhibitor) – treat as urological emergency
  - Hypotension (nitrate interaction, or overdose)
- **Chronic:**
  - Worsening of underlying cardiovascular risk if left untreated (ED is a sentinel for CVD)
  - Psychological distress, relationship breakdown, depression

### Prognosis
- ED itself is not life-threatening; it is a marker for silent ischaemic heart disease and increases all-cause mortality. With treatment (lifestyle + pharmacotherapy), most men achieve adequate erections. Underlying cause (e.g. diabetes) may progress despite treatment.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Ask about morning erections** – if present, psychogenic cause likely; avoid invasive tests.
- **Check testosterone before starting PDE5 inhibitor** – hypogonadism requires replacement, not just PDE5i.
- **Refer all men with sudden onset ED and no clear cause for cardiovascular assessment** – ED may precede MI by 2-3 years.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not prescribe PDE5 inhibitors without checking nitrates** – can cause fatal hypotension.
- **Do not assume all ED is psychogenic** – missing organic causes (diabetes, hypogonadism, vascular disease) delays treatment and increases risk of CVD.
- **Do not start testosterone without ruling out prostate cancer** – PSA, DRE, and risk factors.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** severe headache, visual changes, priapism (erection lasting >4 hours – go to ED), sudden chest pain.
- **Follow-up:** GP in 2-4 weeks to review lifestyle changes and first attempt with medication. Urology/endocrine if no response after 3-4 months of treatment, or if hypogonadism identified.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior:** if suspected cauda equina or spinal cord compression (urgent MRI), pituitary apoplexy (urgent neurosurgery/endocrinology), or priapism (urology on-call).
- **Call ICU:** if PDE5i + nitrate-induced hypotension refractory to fluids; or if pituitary crisis with hypotension, electrolyte disturbance.
- **NEWS2:** use standard track and trigger. ED itself does not cause abnormal vitals; escalate based on underlying condition.
